title: Discovery and control of fire
subtitle: 5.2 Early Human Innovations | Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
era: Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
cause_effect: Cause: Environmental adaptation need. Effect: Allowed cooking, better nutrition, protection from animals, survival in colder climates, and led to social gathering around fire.

title: Palaeolithic age: lifestyle and tools
subtitle: 5.1 The Stone Age | Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
era: Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
cause_effect: Cause: Need to hunt, scavenge, and process food. Effect: Development of flaked stone tools (hand axes, scrapers), nomadic lifestyle, cave art, and early language development.

title: Mesolithic and Neolithic transitions
subtitle: 5.1 The Stone Age | Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
era: Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
cause_effect: Cause: Climate warming after last Ice Age. Effect: Shift from hunting-gathering to food production (domestication of plants and animals), leading to settled village life.

title: Bhimbetka and other rock shelters
subtitle: 5.3 Cave Art and Early Settlements | Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
era: Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat

subtitle: 5.2 Early Human Innovations | Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat
era: Ch 5: India, That Is Bharat

title: Grid patterns and streets at Harappa and Mohenjo-Daro
subtitle: 6.1 Indus Valley City Planning | Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
era: Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
cause_effect: Cause: Need for organized urban living due to surplus agriculture. Effect: Efficient movement, sanitation, and social order; influenced later town planning in India.

title: Advanced drainage and water management systems
subtitle: 6.1 Indus Valley City Planning | Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
era: Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation

title: Granaries and warehouse structures
subtitle: 6.2 Great Bath and Public Structures | Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
era: Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation

title: Trade with Mesopotamia: beads, shells, and metals
subtitle: 6.3 Economic Activities | Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
era: Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
cause_effect: Cause: Surplus production of crafts and need for raw materials (lapis lazuli, copper). Effect: Cultural exchange, spread of Indus seals to Mesopotamia, and economic prosperity.

title: The Great Bath at Mohenjo-Daro: possible ritual use
subtitle: 6.2 Great Bath and Public Structures | Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
era: Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation

title: Theories of decline: climate change, river drying, or invasion
subtitle: 6.5 Script and Decline | Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
era: Ch 6: The Beginnings of Indian Civilisation
cause_effect: Cause: Possible climate change (weakening monsoons), Sarasvati river drying, or Aryan migration theory. Effect: Abandonment of cities, decline of trade, and migration eastward to the Ganges-Yamuna doab.

title: Silk Route and Indian Ocean trade
subtitle: 3.1 Pre-Modern World | Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
era: Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
cause_effect: Cause: Demand for luxury goods (silk, spices, pearls) across civilizations. Effect: Spread of religions (Buddhism, Islam), technologies (paper, compass), and diseases (plague); global interconnection before modern era.

title: Hand-printing (China, Korea, Japan)
subtitle: 5.1 History of Print in Europe | Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World
era: Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World

title: Arrival and influence of Islam
subtitle: 1.5 Religious Developments | Ch 1: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years
era: Ch 1: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years
cause_effect: Cause: Arab conquest of Sindh (Muhammad bin Qasim) and later Turkic invasions. Effect: Introduction of Islamic governance, Sufi traditions, new architecture (mosques, tombs), and cultural synthesis (Indo-Islamic culture).

title: Emergence of Rajput clans
subtitle: 1.3 New Social and Political Groups | Ch 1: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years
era: Ch 1: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years
cause_effect: Cause: Decline of Harsha's empire and Gupta central authority. Effect: Rise of regional kingdoms, bardic traditions, Rajput valor.

title: Early medieval (8th–12th century)

subtitle: 2.1 Rise of Regional Dynasties | Ch 2: New Kings and Kingdoms
era: Ch 2: New Kings and Kingdoms
cause_effect: Cause: Decline of Harsha's empire and Gupta central authority. Effect: Prolonged warfare over Kannauj, weakening of all three dynasties, which later enabled the Ghaznavid invasions.

era: Ch 2: New Kings and Kingdoms
cause_effect: Cause: Chola imperial ambition and devotion to Shiva (Rajaraja Chola). Effect: Symbol of Chola power, patronage of art and bronze sculpture, and a model for later South Indian temple architecture.

subtitle: 5.3 Temple Desecration | Ch 5: Rulers and Buildings
era: Ch 5: Rulers and Buildings
cause_effect: Cause: Mahmud of Ghazni's raids for wealth and religious symbolism. Effect: Weakening of temple patronage, loss of wealth, and long-term memory of invasion in Indian historical narratives.

era: Ch 4: The Mughal Empire
cause_effect: Cause: Invitation by Daulat Khan Lodi and Rana Sanga; weakness of Ibrahim Lodi's army. Effect: Establishment of the Mughal Empire in India, introduction of gunpowder and artillery in North Indian warfare.

era: Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World
cause_effect: Cause: Need for faster book production than hand-copying (scriptoria). Effect: Print Revolution: mass production of books, spread of Reformation ideas (Luther), Renaissance, Scientific Revolution, and rise of public literacy.

title: Nadir Shah's invasion (1739)
subtitle: 10.1 Crisis of Mughal Empire | Ch 10: 18th-Century Political Formations
era: Ch 10: 18th-Century Political Formations
cause_effect: Cause: Weak Mughal administration and Nadir Shah's need for wealth. Effect: Plunder of the Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor diamond, massive loss of Mughal prestige, and further fragmentation into regional kingdoms.

title: Orientalists: William Jones (support for Indian learning)
subtitle: 6.2 Orientalists vs Anglicists | Ch 6: Civilising the 'Native', Educating the Nation
era: Ch 6: Civilising the 'Native', Educating the Nation

title: Battle of Plassey (1757): Siraj-ud-Daulah vs Clive
subtitle: 2.2 Battles of Plassey and Buxar | Ch 2: From Trade to Territory
era: Ch 2: From Trade to Territory
cause_effect: Cause: Conflict between Siraj-ud-Daulah and the East India Company over trade rights and fortification. Robert Clive's conspiracy with Mir Jafar. Effect: Company gained vast revenue from Bengal (diwani rights in 1765), military supremacy, and became a political power in India.

title: Battle of Buxar (1764) and Treaty of Allahabad
subtitle: 2.2 Battles of Plassey and Buxar | Ch 2: From Trade to Territory
era: Ch 2: From Trade to Territory
cause_effect: Cause: Mir Qasim's resistance to Company's trade abuses and alliance with Awadh and Mughal emperor. Effect: Company got diwani rights of Bengal, Bihar, Odisha; control over Mughal emperor; became the dominant power in North India.

title: Anglicists: James Mill and Macaulay (promotion of English)
subtitle: 6.2 Orientalists vs Anglicists | Ch 6: Civilising the 'Native', Educating the Nation
era: Ch 6: Civilising the 'Native', Educating the Nation

title: Lord Cornwallis's system (1793) in Bengal
subtitle: 3.1 Permanent Settlement | Ch 3: Ruling the Countryside
era: Ch 3: Ruling the Countryside
cause_effect: Cause: Need for fixed revenue to ensure Company income and create loyal zamindars. Effect: Zamindars became landlords, peasants reduced to tenants, high rents led to peasant distress and eventual revolts (e.g., Indigo Rebellion, 1859).

title: Lord Wellesley's policy and terms
subtitle: 2.3 Subsidiary Alliance System | Ch 2: From Trade to Territory
era: Ch 2: From Trade to Territory

title: Raja Rammohan Roy's campaign and Regulation XVII (1829)
subtitle: 7.1 Abolition of Sati | Ch 7: Women, Caste and Reform
era: Ch 7: Women, Caste and Reform
cause_effect: Cause: Rammohan Roy's persistent campaign against Sati using scriptures and reason; pressure on Lord William Bentinck. Effect: Sati declared illegal and punishable; began the era of legal social reform in British India.

topic_name: 5.4 Impact on Religion, Society, and Women
subtitle: 5.4 Impact on Religion, Society, and Women | Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World
era: Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World
cause_effect: Cause: Spread of print technology and women's education movements. Effect: Emergence of women's voices in literature (Rashsundari Devi's 'Amar Jiban'), debates on women's rights, widow remarriage, and child marriage.

title: Three periods: Hindu, Muslim, British
subtitle: 1.2 James Mill's Periodization | Ch 1: How, When and Where
era: Ch 1: How, When and Where

title: Luddites and machine-breaking protests
subtitle: 4.2 Factory System and Workers | Ch 4: The Age of Industrialisation
era: Ch 4: The Age of Industrialisation
cause_effect: Cause: Introduction of machinery that replaced skilled workers and lowered wages. Effect: Destroyed machines in protests (named after Ned Ludd); British government sent troops, made machine-breaking a capital crime (1812), but ultimately industrialization continued.

subtitle: 7.2 Widow Remarriage | Ch 7: Women, Caste and Reform
era: Ch 7: Women, Caste and Reform
cause_effect: Cause: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's campaign based on Vedic texts. Effect: Legalized widow remarriage, but social stigma continued; gradually changed orthodox Hindu practices.

title: End of East India Company rule (1858)
subtitle: 5.7 Consequences | Ch 5: When People Rebel - 1857
era: Ch 5: When People Rebel - 1857
cause_effect: Cause: British failure to suppress 1857 rebellion quickly; realization that Company rule was unstable. Effect: British Crown took direct control (Raj), Queen's Proclamation promised religious tolerance and no further annexations; reorganization of army.

title: Reorganization of army and policies toward princes
subtitle: 5.7 Consequences | Ch 5: When People Rebel - 1857

title: Forest Acts and loss of traditional rights
subtitle: 4.2 Colonial Impact on Tribals | Ch 4: Tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age
era: Ch 4: Tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age
cause_effect: Cause: Colonial need for timber (railways, ships) and commercial crops. Effect: Forced eviction of tribals from forests, ban on shifting cultivation, and led directly to tribal revolts (Santhal, Birsa Munda, Bastar).

title: Victor Emmanuel II proclaimed king (1861)
subtitle: 1.4 Unification of Italy | Ch 1: Rise of Nationalism in Europe
era: Ch 1: Rise of Nationalism in Europe
cause_effect: Cause: Rise of nationalist secret societies (Young Italy, Carbonari); failure of 1848 revolutions. Effect: Italy unified; Rome annexed in 1870 after Franco-Prussian War but faced North-South divide.

title: Proclamation of German Empire (1871)
subtitle: 1.3 Unification of Germany | Ch 1: Rise of Nationalism in Europe
era: Ch 1: Rise of Nationalism in Europe
cause_effect: Cause: Prussian desire for German unification under conservative leadership (kleindeutsch), excluding Austria. Effect: German Empire proclaimed at Versailles; shifted European balance of power; contributed to tensions leading to WWI.

title: Jyotirao Phule and Satyashodhak Samaj
subtitle: 7.4 Challenging Caste System | Ch 7: Women, Caste and Reform
era: Ch 7: Women, Caste and Reform
cause_effect: Cause: Phule's own experience of caste discrimination and denial of education. Effect: Founded Satyashodhak Samaj to challenge Brahmanical supremacy; opened schools for Shudras and Ati-Shudras; influenced later Dalit movements.

title: Censorship and Vernacular Press Act (1878)
subtitle: 5.3 Print, Public Debate, and Politics | Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World
era: Ch 5: Print Culture and the Modern World
cause_effect: Cause: British fear of nationalist writings in vernacular newspapers. Effect: Tight censorship of Indian-language press, heavy fines and confiscation of printing presses; led to stronger underground pamphleteering and nationalist journalism.

title: Indian National Congress (1885) foundation
subtitle: 8.1 Emergence of Nationalism | Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
era: Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement

subtitle: 4.3 Tribal Revolts | Ch 4: Tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age
era: Ch 4: Tribals, Dikus and the Vision of a Golden Age
cause_effect: Cause: Forest Acts, land alienation, forced labor, and missionary activities (Direct cause: Birsa's divine claim and Munda uprising). Effect: British created the Chhotanagpur Tenancy Act (1908) to protect tribal land; inspired future tribal movements.

title: Lord Curzon's partition of Bengal
subtitle: 8.2 Partition of Bengal (1905) | Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
era: Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
cause_effect: Cause: Curzon's stated administrative reason; actual cause: divide and rule policy (Hindu-Muslim rift). Effect: Massive Swadeshi and Boycott movement, rise of revolutionary terrorism, and eventual annulment of partition in 1911 (capital shifted to Delhi).

title: Treaty of Versailles (1919) harsh terms
subtitle: 3.1 Aftermath of World War I | Ch 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
era: Ch 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
cause_effect: Cause: Allied victory in WWI and desire to punish Germany. Effect: Germany lost territory, had to pay massive reparations, and accepted war guilt; led directly to economic crisis, hyperinflation (1923), and resentment exploited by Hitler.

title: Jallianwala Bagh massacre (13 April 1919)
subtitle: 2.2 Rowlatt Act and Jallianwala Bagh | Ch 2: Nationalism in India
era: Ch 2: Nationalism in India
cause_effect: Cause: Rowlatt Act allowed detention without trial; protest meeting at Jallianwala Bagh. Direct cause: General Dyer ordered firing on unarmed crowd. Effect: Nationwide outrage; Rabindranath Tagore returned knighthood; Gandhi withdrew from cooperation with British; non-cooperation movement emerged.

title: Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22)
subtitle: 2.3 Khilafat and Non-Cooperation | Ch 2: Nationalism in India
era: Ch 2: Nationalism in India
cause_effect: Cause: Jallianwala Bagh massacre (1919) and injustice to Turkey's Caliph (Khilafat issue). Effect: Widespread boycott of British goods, courts, schools; surrender of titles; movement withdrawn after Chauri Chaura (1922).

title: Dandi March (12 March 1930)
subtitle: 2.4 Civil Disobedience and Salt Satyagraha | Ch 2: Nationalism in India
era: Ch 2: Nationalism in India
cause_effect: Cause: Failure of Simon Commission (1928), rejection of Nehru Report (1928), and Lahore Congress demand for Purna Swaraj (1929). Direct cause: British salt monopoly. Effect: Massive civil disobedience, 60,000+ arrested, Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931), and participation of women and lower castes in movement.
geo_location: Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi, Gujarat, India

title: 1933: Hitler becomes Chancellor
subtitle: 3.2 Rise of the Nazi Party | Ch 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
era: Ch 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
cause_effect: Cause: Great Depression (1929), fear of communism, and political maneuvering by conservative elites. Effect: Enabling Act (1933) gave Hitler dictatorial powers, banned other parties, established totalitarian Nazi state, began persecution of Jews.

title: 'Quit India' resolution (August 1942)
subtitle: 2.7 Quit India Movement | Ch 2: Nationalism in India
era: Ch 2: Nationalism in India
cause_effect: Cause: Failure of Cripps Mission (1942), fear of Japanese invasion during WWII, and British refusal to grant immediate self-rule. Effect: Massive arrests (Gandhi, Nehru, all leaders), underground movements, parallel governments (Ballia, Tamluk), but movement suppressed by 1944; hastened British decision to quit after WWII.

title: Underground activities and parallel governments
subtitle: 8.6 Quit India Movement (1942) | Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
era: Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
geo_location: Various parts of India (Ballia, Tamluk, Satara)

title: Hitler's suicide and Germany's surrender (1945)
subtitle: 3.6 Downfall of Hitler | Ch 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
era: Ch 3: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler
cause_effect: Cause: Allied advance from east (Soviet Union) and west (USA, UK, France); Battle of Stalingrad (1942-43) defeat. Effect: End of World War II in Europe, division of Germany into East and West, and beginning of the Cold War.

title: Partition violence and refugees
subtitle: 8.9 Independence and Partition | Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
era: Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement
cause_effect: Cause: Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947) announced Partition of India on religious lines. Effect: One of the largest mass migrations in history (14 million people), communal violence (500,000–2 million deaths), and lasting India-Pakistan hostility (Kashmir conflict).

title: India's independence (15 August 1947)
subtitle: 8.9 Independence and Partition | Ch 8: The Making of the National Movement

title: Storming of the Bastille (14 July 1789)
subtitle: 1.4 Outbreak of the Revolution | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: Famine, unemployment, fear of royal troops, and search for gunpowder. Effect: Symbol of popular uprising; led to the abolition of feudalism (4 August 1789) and the Declaration of Rights of Man (26 August 1789).

title: Great Fear in the countryside
subtitle: 1.4 Outbreak of the Revolution | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution

title: 'Men are born free and equal'
subtitle: 1.5 Declaration of Rights of Man | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: The storming of the Bastille and popular pressure on the National Assembly. Effect: Established constitutional principles of liberty, property, and resistance to oppression; inspired future democratic movements worldwide.

title: Women's march to Versailles
subtitle: 1.7 Role of Women | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution

title: Olympe de Gouges and Declaration of Rights of Women
subtitle: 1.7 Role of Women | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution

title: Execution of Louis XVI (1793)
subtitle: 1.6 France Becomes a Republic | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: Discovery of Louis XVI's secret correspondence with foreign powers; fear of counter-revolution. Effect: Radicalization of the Revolution, entry into Reign of Terror (1793-94) under Robespierre, and foreign wars against monarchical Europe.

title: Reign of Terror under Robespierre
subtitle: 1.6 France Becomes a Republic | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution

title: Directory and political instability
subtitle: 1.6 France Becomes a Republic | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution

title: Coup of 18 Brumaire (1799)
subtitle: 1.8 Rise of Napoleon | Ch 1: The French Revolution
era: Ch 1: The French Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: Political instability of the Directory, military failures, and popular desire for strong leadership. Effect: Napoleon became First Consul, ended the Revolution, established the Napoleonic Code, and expanded French control across Europe.

title: Napoleonic Code and administrative reforms
subtitle: 1.8 Rise of Napoleon | Ch 1: The French Revolution

title: Colonialism: raw materials to Europe, finished goods to colonies
subtitle: 3.2 19th Century Global Economy | Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
era: Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
cause_effect: Cause: Industrial Revolution created demand for raw cotton, indigo, tea, and market for machine-made goods. Effect: Deindustrialization of India (textile collapse), commercialization of agriculture (opium to China, indigo in Bengal), and global economic inequality.

title: Post-World War I economic collapse
subtitle: 3.3 Inter-War Economy | Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
era: Ch 3: The Making of a Global World

title: Great Depression (1929-1930s) and impact on India
subtitle: 3.3 Inter-War Economy | Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
era: Ch 3: The Making of a Global World
cause_effect: Cause: US stock market crash (1929), overproduction, agricultural price collapse. Effect: Indian agricultural prices fell drastically, peasants indebted, demand for Indian goods fell, but India became a major gold exporter; contributed to civil disobedience movement.

title: Deindustrialization of Indian textiles
subtitle: 4.3 Industrialization in Colonies | Ch 4: The Age of Industrialisation
era: Ch 4: The Age of Industrialisation
cause_effect: Cause: British industrial revolution produced cheap machine-made cloth; high tariffs on Indian cloth in Britain. Effect: Collapse of Indian handloom industry, massive unemployment among weavers, and shift of India from cloth exporter to raw cotton exporter.

title: Indian mills (Bombay, Ahmedabad) and competition

era: Ch 4: The Age of Industrialisation

title: Collectivization of agriculture and kulak persecution
subtitle: 2.7 Stalinism | Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
era: Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: Need to industrialize rapidly and extract grain for urban workers. Effect: Forced consolidation of farms into collective farms (kolkhozy), resistance from kulaks, famine in Ukraine (Holodomor, 1932-33), and millions of deaths.

title: Food shortages and strikes in Petrograd
subtitle: 2.4 February Revolution (1917) | Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
era: Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution

title: Abdication of Tsar and Provisional Government
subtitle: 2.4 February Revolution (1917) | Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
era: Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: World War I casualties, food and fuel shortages, Tsar's absence at war front. Effect: Tsar Nicholas II abdicated (2 March 1917); Provisional Government established, but continued war and land reforms delayed.

title: Lenin's April Theses ('Peace, Land, Bread')
subtitle: 2.5 October Revolution (1917) | Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
era: Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution

title: Bolshevik takeover of Winter Palace
subtitle: 2.5 October Revolution (1917) | Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
era: Ch 2: Socialism in Europe and Russian Revolution
cause_effect: Cause: Provisional Government's failure to end war or distribute land; Lenin's April Theses ('Peace, Land, Bread'). Effect: Bolsheviks seized power, Lenin as head of state, Russia withdrew from World War I (Treaty of Brest-Litovsk, 1918), and began civil war.
